reasoning: The 22 nouns reveal 5 common patterns for plural formation: o (identical forms, e.g., dɛl-dɛl); a (vowel a to e/ɛ with reduction, e.g., gaar-géar, ŋàak-ŋɛk, maac-mɛ́c); n (nasal alternation/addition, e.g., daŋ-dɛn, diar-njær, ájwɔ̤n-ájɛn); l (vowel lengthening, e.g., gál-gaál, ljáar-laáj); c (consonant modification like w-deletion or b-r substitution, e.g., twɔ̤ŋ-tɔŋ, bòok-ròok, adjéel-adíl). For N1, only (b) mìit (firefly, animate; follows a like fire maac-mɛ́c, long ii to ɛ, plural mɛt) and (f) tùng (horn; follows c w-deletion reverse like egg tɔŋ from twɔ̤ŋ, singular twùng) fit a common pattern clearly. Others lack close matches or lead to ambiguity without violating example structures.
The 1st person lwɔ̀j follows the pattern of root kuc to 1st kwɔ̀c (insert w after initial, u to ɔ̀); reverse: remove w, ɔ̀ to u, yielding root luj.
For this verb pattern (type 2 like 'to not know'), 3rd person = root = luj.
The 3rd person cɛm follows the pattern where 3rd = root (regular for types 1,2,4); root = cɛm.
Root/3rd cɛm has ɛ like lɛk-laak (type 4, ɛ to aa); 1st caam.
Root/1st pɛɛc has long ɛɛ (unlike short ɛ in type 4); fits regular where forms stay same for long vowels (extension of type 3 same for 1st/root), 3rd = pɛɛc.
Root/3rd wic has i (front high like ɛ mid in type 4); extend vowel opening/lengthening to aa, 1st waac.
3rd bók follows regular pattern where 3rd = root; root = bók.
Root/3rd bók has ó (back rounded like u in type 2); insert w after initial, keep ó (close to u-ɔ̀ change but tone high preserved), 1st bwók.
Plural rim follows c pattern (initial substitution like bòok-ròok, b to r, stem òok same); reverse: r to b, stem im same, singular bim.
Singular will (inanimate like plant wál-wál) follows o pattern (identical); plural will.
Singular ljáar (for buffalo, like animal ljáar-laáj) follows l pattern (vowel lengthening a to aa, j adjustment); plural laáj.
Singular rɛɛc (inanimate like path dɛl-dɛl) follows o pattern (identical); plural rɛɛc.
Plural kaal follows l pattern (lengthening like gál-gaál, short á to aa); singular kál.
Singular kàk (a...k structure) follows a pattern (a to e, like ŋàak-ŋɛk, kàk-kek); plural kek.
Singular ráan (aa n structure) follows a pattern (aa to ɛ, reduce like maac-mɛ́c or daŋ-dɛn); plural rɛn.
Plural leek (...eek) follows a pattern reverse (e to a, like ŋɛk from ŋàak; ee k from aa k); singular laak.
Singular ról (inanimate like python nɛ́l-nɛ́l) follows o pattern (identical); plural ról.
Singular jít (inanimate like louse nók-nòk, tone aside) follows o pattern (identical); plural jít.

answer: Dinka nouns exhibit 5 common plural formation patterns analogous to verb conjugation classes: o (identical S/P for some inanimates); a (stem vowel a/aa to e/ɛ, often reducing final syllable, e.g., for ak/ar endings); n (nasal-related, adding/changing to n in P, vowel often to ɛ, for animates/inanimates); l (vowel lengthening, e.g., short a to aa); c (consonant modification, e.g., delete w/j or substitute b-r). Verbs show parallel changes in 1st person (e.g., ɛ to aa, insert w with u to ɔ̀, nasal ŋ to n with i to a; some identical or suppletive in 3rd), enabling noun class grouping for prediction despite apparent randomness.
